description: The pelagic thresher is the smallest of the three thresher shark species and the most fully oceanic — it spends nearly its entire life in the open tropical and subtropical Indo-Pacific, rarely approaching coastlines. Like all threshers, it is defined by its extraordinary elongated upper tail lobe, which can equal or exceed half its total body length and is used to herd and stun schooling prey. The pelagic thresher is among the most heavily impacted shark species in the Indo-Pacific: populations are estimated to have declined by more than 70% in parts of its range over three generations, driven almost entirely by longline bycatch from tuna and swordfish fisheries centered in the Philippines, Indonesia, and Taiwan.
funFact: Pelagic threshers are among the few open-ocean sharks known to visit coral reef cleaning stations — despite spending their lives far out to sea, they regularly swim into shallow reef areas where cleaner wrasses remove parasites from their skin, gills, and even inside their mouths, then return to open water.
habitat: Open tropical and subtropical Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea and East Africa eastward through the Indian Ocean to Japan, Hawaii, Australia, and the eastern Pacific. Nearly entirely pelagic — found in the upper water column from the surface to around 150 m, with occasional deeper dives. Rarely enters shelf or coastal waters except at reef cleaning stations.
diet: Primarily small schooling fish such as sardines, anchovies, and small tuna, as well as squid and cuttlefish. Like other threshers, it uses rapid lateral tail-slap strikes to stun prey before consuming them. Hunts in the upper water column, often near the surface at night.
size: Typically 2.5–3.0 m (8.2–9.8 ft) total length including tail; tail lobe can equal roughly half the total length. Maximum confirmed ~3.3 m (10.8 ft). Considerably smaller and more slender than the common thresher.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: No reliable global estimate — >30% decline globally; >70% decline in parts of Indo-Pacific range

vitalSigns.label: Top Speed
vitalSigns.value: ~40 km/h (~25 mph) estimated during prey strike

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: 2 pups per litter (one per uterus); ~12 month gestation; adelphophagy (embryos nourished by unfertilized eggs in utero)

vitalSigns.label: Age at Maturity
vitalSigns.value: ~8–10 years; among the slowest-reproducing threshers relative to body size

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Sardines, anchovies, small tuna, squid, cuttlefish; hunts with tail-slap strikes in the upper water column

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Very low — extremely small litters (only 2 pups), long gestation, and late maturity make population recovery exceptionally slow

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Elongated caudal fin (upper lobe ~50% of total length) used as a whip to stun schooling prey; slender, hydrodynamic body built for open-ocean endurance

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical and subtropical Indo-Pacific; Red Sea and East Africa east to Japan, Hawaii, Australia, and Eastern Pacific

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to ~150 m (490 ft); predominantly upper water column

threats.name: Pelagic Longline Bycatch
threats.description: By far the dominant threat. The pelagic thresher is one of the most frequently caught sharks in Indo-Pacific longline fisheries targeting yellowfin tuna and swordfish. Its open-ocean surface range overlaps directly with dense longline effort across the western and central Pacific.

threats.name: Targeted Fishing for Fins
threats.description: Despite being caught primarily as bycatch, fins are retained at high rates throughout the Philippines, Indonesia, and Taiwan — the epicenter of its range — where fin demand and minimal enforcement drive intensive take.

threats.name: Drift Net Fisheries
threats.description: Large-scale drift nets, historically banned on the high seas but still used illegally and legally in some national waters, entangle pelagic threshers at high rates given their surface-oriented behavior.

threats.name: Extremely Low Reproductive Rate
threats.description: With a maximum of 2 pups per litter and multi-year reproductive cycles, the pelagic thresher cannot recover quickly from fishing pressure. Even modest removal rates above natural mortality can cause long-term population decline.

threats.name: Inadequate Management
threats.description: CITES Appendix II listing since 2016 has not been consistently enforced across its range. RFMO measures in the Pacific address tuna and swordfish, not bycatch species. National management in the Philippines and Indonesia — where most fishing occurs — remains weak.

actionItems.title: Choose Sustainable Seafood
actionItems.description: Avoid tuna and swordfish caught by pelagic longlines in the Indo-Pacific without independent observer coverage or Marine Stewardship Council certification. Use the Monterey Bay Aquarium Seafood Watch guide.

actionItems.title: Support CITES Appendix II Enforcement
actionItems.description: Advocate for stronger national implementation of CITES trade restrictions on thresher shark fins and products, particularly in the Philippines, Indonesia, and Taiwan where the fin trade is most active.

actionItems.title: Avoid Shark Products
actionItems.description: Do not purchase shark fin, shark liver oil, or other shark-derived products. Consumer demand for shark fin in Asia is the direct economic driver sustaining the markets that make bycatch retention profitable.

actionItems.title: Support Indo-Pacific Shark Research
actionItems.description: Organizations like the Save Our Seas Foundation and WWF fund observer programs and research in the Indo-Pacific that are essential for generating the stock data needed to build enforceable catch limits.

healthMetrics.label: IUCN Status
healthMetrics.value: Endangered — listed since 2007; population estimated to have declined >30% globally and >70% in parts of the Indo-Pacific

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — significant CPUE declines documented in Philippine, Indonesian, and Taiwanese longline fishery data

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — open ocean habitat largely intact but warming and deoxygenation affecting prey distribution and distribution ranges

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — sardines, anchovies, and squid heavily depleted in key Indo-Pacific fishing zones

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Critical — among the most frequently landed sharks in Indo-Pacific longline and gillnet fisheries; fins routinely retained

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Minimal — CITES Appendix II since 2016; no binding catch limits from any RFMO; national protections absent in core range states

populationTrendMeta.note: No reliable global population estimate exists. Values are loosely modeled from documented CPUE declines in Indo-Pacific longline fishery data, particularly from the Philippines, Taiwan, and Indonesian observer programs. The IUCN cites a decline exceeding 30% globally over three generations, with regional declines in excess of 70% in heavily fished parts of the range. Absolute numbers are highly speculative and intended to illustrate trajectory, not precise abundance.

preyDeclineRegions.name: W. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Sardines, anchovies, and squid severely depleted in Philippine and Indonesian waters by intensive artisanal and industrial fisheries

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indian Ocean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Schooling prey fish under heavy pressure from tuna purse-seine and longline operations; squid populations declining

preyDeclineRegions.name: C. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Open-ocean prey relatively less depleted; some pressure from industrial tuna fishing on forage fish

preyDeclineRegions.name: E. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Anchovy and sardine populations variable; prey availability reduced in core upwelling zones near historical range edges

fishingPressureRegions.name: W. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Dense longline effort targeting yellowfin tuna from Philippine, Indonesian, Taiwanese, and Japanese fleets; highest observed pelagic thresher landing rates globally

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indian Ocean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Extensive longline and gillnet operations; limited observer coverage; fins routinely retained and traded through regional hubs

fishingPressureRegions.name: C. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Industrial tuna longlines active throughout range; some WCPFC oversight but bycatch limits not species-specific

fishingPressureRegions.name: E. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Longlines targeting swordfish and tuna; IATTC manages tuna but has no dedicated pelagic thresher provisions
